food_subgroup: Cephalopod seafood dishes — grilled or sautéed squid on a hot plate

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for hot-plate or teppan-style prepared squid dish; closest parent 'squid food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a commodity group; no specific code exists for hot-plate prepared squid dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for hot-plate squid as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of hot plate squid is approximately 200 g, typically presented sizzling on a cast-iron or stone plate with sauce and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175177); adapted with Korean seasoning sauce contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for gochujang and soy sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base squid values taken from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (FDC ID 175177). Sodium and carbohydrate values adjusted upward to reflect typical Korean hot-plate seasoning (gochujang, soy sauce, sugar, garlic) based on USDA FoodData Central entries for those condiments. Energy recalculated from adjusted macros using Atwater factors. This is an adaptation; exact restaurant formulation is unknown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot-plate squid. Squid is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood. GI estimated at 40 by analogy with other seasoned seafood dishes with moderate sauce carbohydrate contribution, consistent with the low-GI category for protein-dominant seafood preparations as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The sauce (gochujang, sugar) contributes modest carbohydrate, keeping the overall dish GI in the low range. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 × 14.4) / 100 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and gochujang seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid hot plate squid due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1040 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation may lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from gochujang and sugar in seasoning sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate squid has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 40) and moderate glycemic load per serving (GL approximately 5.8), making it a relatively acceptable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the sugar content in the sauce should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content in squid and high sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 195 mg per 100 g) and the dish carries significant sodium from Korean seasoning; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer preparations with reduced sauce to limit sodium and cholesterol burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate Squid (石板烤鱿鱼; Korean: 철판 오징어, Cheolpan Ojingeo) is a Korean-style seafood dish in which cleaned whole or sliced squid is cooked and served sizzling on a preheated cast-iron or stone hot plate. The squid is typically marinated or coated in a spicy-sweet Korean sauce based on gochujang (fermented chilli paste), soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and sugar, then grilled or sautéed until lightly charred and aromatic. The dish is characterised by a tender yet slightly chewy texture, a bold umami-spicy-sweet flavour profile, and the dramatic sizzling presentation at the table. In Singapore, it appears on Korean restaurant menus as a main course or shared side dish, often accompanied by steamed rice and banchan (Korean side dishes). Nutritionally, hot plate squid is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-to-moderate carbohydrate dish. Squid provides lean protein, vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and riboflavin. The seasoning sauce contributes sodium, sugar, and modest carbohydrate. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (approximately 40) but carries a high sodium load from the sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The cholesterol content of squid is naturally elevated and should be noted for cardiovascular risk management. Overall, it is a nutritionally dense seafood option when consumed in moderate portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot plate squid?
Is hot plate squid high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat hot plate squid?
Hot plate squid GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Korean hot-plate squid exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid with upward adjustment for Korean seasoning sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked squid (FDC ID 175177) and is not reliably documented for this preparation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked squid — squid contains no industrially produced trans fats and the value is confirmed zero by the source. Sodium value of 520 mg per 100 g reflects adaptation for Korean seasoning sauce; plain cooked squid per USDA SR Legacy is approximately 260 mg per 100 g. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate for a protein-dominant seasoned seafood dish; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GL per serving = (40 × 14.4 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8. The food is listed under Korean cuisine at a Korean restaurant in Singapore (Mincheng Bibimbap, Funan); the Chinese name 石板烤鱿鱼 appears in the original field survey listing and is preserved. Halal status is assumed based on seafood classification but cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid base adapted for Korean seasoning. Added Korean local name 철판 오징어 to satisfy G6. Added Chinese names from source listing. Set GI to 40 (analogous estimate, low category). Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel. Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 confirmed zero. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.

Iteration 2: Verified all hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria. Confirmed GI category 'low' correct for GI 40 (≤ 55 threshold). Confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Confirmed zero-macro justification not required (no core macro is zero). Confirmed aliases consistency. Final decision PASS.
